messages.content:
1. Original: I don't got no money.
   Polished: I don't have any money.

2. Original: Me and him went to the store.
   Polished: He and I went to the store.

3. Original: Ain't nobody got time for that.
   Polished: Nobody has time for that.

4. Original: I seen that movie before.
   Polished: I have seen that movie before.

5. Original: I ain't doing nothing tonight.
   Polished: I am not doing anything tonight.
The staining results showed that the in vivo injection of mir-195 had some degree of myelin protection in almost all segments

of the spinal cord.

Polished: The staining results indicate that the in vivo injection of mir-195 provided a significant degree of myelin protection across almost all segments of the spinal cord.
The results of immunostaining using anti-MBP showed that in vivo injection of miR-195-5p agomir provided a significant preservation of myelin sheath across almost all segments

of the spinal cord, as compared to the control group.

Polished: The results of immunostaining with anti-MBP revealed that the in vivo injection of miR-195-5p agomir resulted in a significant preservation of myelin sheath across nearly all segments of the spinal cord, in comparison to the control group.

The results of immunostaining using anti-MBP showed that in vivo injection of miR-195-5p agomir provided a significant preservation of myelin sheath across nearly all segments of the injuried sciatic nerve, in comparison to the control group

.

Polished: Immunostaining results using anti-MBP demonstrated that the administration of miR-195-5p agomir via in vivo injection significantly preserved the myelin sheath across almost all segments of the damaged sciatic nerve, when compared to the control group.

Sciatic nerve longitude sections were further immunostained with NF200, a marker of neuronal axons. Rat injected with miR-195-5p agomir, as compared with agomir control, exhibited relatively stronger intensity of NF200 and longer regenerated length of axons at 3 days following nerve injury (Figure 4E&F). These findings imply that after peripheral nerve injury, miR-195-5p modulates distribution of Schwann cells in vivo, affects myelin preservation and stimulates the regrowth and elongation of axons.

To further analyze sciatic nerve sections longitudinally, they were immunostained with NF200 which is a marker for neuronal axons. Rats that received in vivo injections of miR-195-5p agomir showed significantly higher NF200 intensity and longer regenerated length of axons after 3 days of nerve injury compared to the agomir control (Figure 4E&F). These results suggest that miR-195-5p has an impact on Schwann cell distribution, as well as on myelin preservation after peripheral nerve injury, and furthermore stimulates the growth and elongation of axons.

Our results show that a large number of Schwann cell were gathered and distributed in the proximal nerve near the injury site, while myelin protein levels were significantly reduced. However, at the site of injury, the level of myelin protein increases. According to our speculation, the loss of myelin protein expression may be due to dedifferentiation of Schwann cells in the proximal nerve, while the migrated Schwann cells begin to redifferentiate, resulting in an increase in myelin protein expression at the injury site. However, there is a significant amount of myelin debris in the injured segment and distal nerve stumps at 3 days after injury.

Our research findings reveal that immediately following injury, a large proportion of Schwann cells accumulate and distribute themselves in the proximal nerve region close to the site of injury, leading to a noticeable decrease in levels of myelin protein. In contrast, at the injury site itself, levels of myelin protein are observed to increase. Based on our analysis, we speculate that the loss of myelin protein expression can be attributed to the dedifferentiation of Schwann cells in the proximal nerve region, whereby migrating Schwann cells begin to redifferentiate, resulting in heightened myelin protein expression at the injury site. However, it is worth noting that there exists a significant amount of myelin debris in both the injured segment and distal nerve stumps even at 3 days post-injury.

According to our speculation, the loss of myelin protein expression may be due to dedifferentiation of Schwann cells in the proximal nerve, while the migrated Schwann cells begin to redifferentiate, resulting in an increase in myelin protein expression at the injury site. In additionHowever, there is a significant amount of myelin debris in the injured segment and distal nerve stumps at 3 days after injury.

Polished: Our hypothesis is that the decline in myelin protein expression may be attributed to the dedifferentiation of Schwann cells in the proximal nerve region, while the Schwann cells that migrate begin to redifferentiate, leading to an increase in myelin protein expression at the site of injury. Nonetheless, we found that there exists a remarkable amount of myelin debris in both the injured section and distal nerve stumps even at 3 days post-injury.

According to our speculation, the loss of myelin protein expression may be due to dedifferentiation of Schwann cells in the proximal nerve, while the migrated Schwann cells begin to redifferentiate, resulting in an increase in myelin protein expression at the injury site. In addition, there is a significant amount of myelin debris in the injured segment and distal nerve stumps at 3 days after injury.

Polished: Our hypothesis suggests that the decrease in myelin protein expression could be attributed to the dedifferentiation of Schwann cells in the proximal nerve, with migrating Schwann cells commencing a process of redifferentiation that leads to an increase in myelin protein expression at the site of injury. At the same time, we observed a substantial accumulation of myelin debris in the injured segment and distal nerve stumps even after 3 days post-injury.

Our results show that a large number of Schwann cells were gathered and distributed in the proximal nerve near the injury site, while myelin protein levels were significantly reduced. However, at the injury site, the level of myelin protein increases.

Polished: Our findings indicate that following nerve injury, there was aggregation and dispersal of a significant number of Schwann cells in the proximal nerve area adjacent to the site of injury, with accompanying reductions in myelin protein levels. Notably, at the injury site itself, there was an observed increase in myelin protein expression.
